meta.title: California Life Insurance Exam Bank (20 Batches, 400 Questions)
notes: Generated bank with varied stems, formats (MCQ + select-all-that-apply), and balanced answer-letter distribution.
Not legal/tax advice; exam-style general principles. Verify against your course/CA DOI materials.

questions.question: Which policy provision states that the policy (and attached application/riders, if any) constitutes the full agreement between insurer and owner?

choices.C: Entire contract provision
choices.D: Grace period provision

questions.rationale: The entire contract clause limits the agreement to the written policy and included documents—not verbal promises.

questions.question: Which feature is MOST commonly guaranteed in traditional whole life insurance?

choices.A: A guaranteed cash value schedule and level premium per the contract
choices.B: Premiums that decrease automatically each year
choices.C: Guaranteed dividends at a fixed rate
choices.D: Market-based returns that cannot decline

questions.rationale: Whole life typically guarantees level premiums and cash values; dividends (if any) are generally not guaranteed.

questions.question: A client wants the lowest-cost temporary death benefit protection for 20 years (income replacement while children are young). What is the BEST fit?

choices.C: Variable life for growth potential
choices.D: Whole life with paid-up additions

questions.rationale: Level term is usually the lowest-cost way to buy a large temporary death benefit for a defined period.

questions.question: A universal life (UL) policyowner pays the minimum premium for years. COI charges rise with age and credited interest falls. What is the most likely risk?

choices.A: The insurer must reduce charges to match the premium
choices.C: The policy becomes paid-up automatically at year 10
choices.D: Cash value may be depleted and the policy can lapse if funding is insufficient

questions.rationale: UL sustainability depends on funding, charges, and crediting; underfunding can lead to lapse.

questions.question: Which disclosure is MOST important for a variable life policy?

choices.C: Cash value (and some death benefit features) can fluctuate with market performance; investment risk is borne by the owner
choices.D: Cash value is guaranteed not to decline

questions.rationale: Variable products are securities-like; values fluctuate and risk is shifted to the policyowner.

questions.question: A participating whole life policy pays dividends. If the owner chooses 'paid-up additions,' what is the most likely effect?

choices.B: Increases cash value and death benefit over time by purchasing additional permanent insurance
choices.C: Guarantees dividends in the future
choices.D: Converts the policy to term automatically

questions.rationale: Paid-up additions use dividends to buy extra permanent coverage, increasing both cash value and death benefit.

questions.question: A whole life policyowner stops paying premiums and wants to keep the same face amount for as long as possible using existing cash value. Which nonforfeiture option fits BEST?

choices.C: Reduced paid-up insurance
choices.D: Extended term insurance

questions.rationale: Extended term uses cash value to buy term coverage for the original face amount for a limited time.

questions.question: A waiver of premium rider typically does what when the insured becomes totally disabled (per rider definition)?

choices.A: Converts the policy to an annuity immediately
choices.B: Waives premiums after an elimination period to keep the policy in force
choices.C: Doubles the death benefit automatically
choices.D: Eliminates the suicide clause

questions.rationale: Waiver riders generally waive required premiums after a waiting (elimination) period during qualifying disability.

questions.question: To start a life insurance claim, which items are most commonly required? (Select all that apply.)

choices.C: New medical exam of the beneficiary
choices.D: Additional documentation depending on circumstances (e.g., accident reports)

questions.rationale: Claims generally require a claim form and certified death certificate; additional documents may be needed depending on the loss.

questions.question: An agent offers to pay a client's first premium if the client buys today. This is most likely:

choices.C: A permissible discount in all cases
choices.D: An illegal rebate/inducement (rebating)

questions.rationale: Providing something of value contingent on purchase (like paying premium) is commonly prohibited as rebating/inducement.

questions.question: A policy is assigned as collateral for a bank loan. If the insured dies with the debt outstanding, what happens?

choices.A: The agent distributes proceeds at discretion
choices.B: The lender is paid up to the debt amount first; the remainder is paid to the beneficiary
choices.C: The beneficiary is paid first and the lender receives nothing
choices.D: The insurer keeps the proceeds

questions.rationale: Collateral assignments give the lender priority up to the amount owed; the beneficiary receives the remainder.

questions.question: Life insurance death proceeds are generally income-tax-free to beneficiaries, but which item is typically taxable?

choices.B: The beneficiary's relationship to the insured
choices.C: Interest earned if proceeds are held by the insurer under an interest option
choices.D: The face amount paid as a lump sum

questions.rationale: The death benefit is generally income-tax-free; however, interest earned on held proceeds is typically taxable.

questions.question: For a nonqualified deferred annuity, withdrawals before annuitization are generally taxed how?

choices.C: Never taxable
choices.D: Basis-first (FIFO) until basis is withdrawn

questions.rationale: Nonqualified annuity withdrawals are commonly treated as LIFO (earnings first), making early withdrawals taxable.

questions.question: Two partners want funds so the surviving partner can buy the deceased partner’s business interest. What is the best solution?

choices.A: A buy-sell agreement funded by life insurance
choices.C: Credit life insurance
choices.D: Interest-only settlement option

questions.rationale: Buy-sell funding provides liquidity for ownership transfer on death.

questions.question: If a life insurance policy becomes a Modified Endowment Contract (MEC), the main practical impact is:

choices.B: Loans/withdrawals are taxed less favorably (often LIFO) and may incur additional penalties if under 59½
choices.C: Premiums become deductible
choices.D: Death benefit becomes ordinary income

questions.rationale: MEC status changes taxation of distributions; death benefit is generally still income-tax-free.

questions.question: Cameron wants to buy a life insurance policy on Morgan, who is not related and has no business relationship. What is the most likely underwriting/legal issue?

choices.A: The policy is automatically valid if premiums are paid annually
choices.B: The policy would be valid because insurable interest is only checked at claim time
choices.C: Lack of insurable interest at inception (wagering-type arrangement)
choices.D: The only issue is the beneficiary designation being revocable

questions.rationale: Life insurance generally requires insurable interest at inception; otherwise it resembles a wagering contract.

questions.question: Jordan is considering replacing an existing life policy with a new one because an agent says it's 'cheaper.' What is the BEST next step to reduce harm and stay compliant?

choices.A: Backdate the new policy to eliminate contestability
choices.B: Provide required replacement notices/comparisons and avoid canceling the old policy until the new policy is issued, delivered, and in force
choices.C: Promise guaranteed savings to help the client decide
choices.D: Surrender the old policy immediately to avoid paying two premiums

questions.rationale: Replacements are regulated and can create gaps; proper disclosures and sequencing (new coverage in force first) are key.

questions.question: Morgan names a minor child as the sole beneficiary with no trust/custodian named. If Morgan dies while the child is still a minor, what is the most likely complication?

choices.A: The minor automatically receives and manages proceeds immediately with full legal capacity
choices.B: The agent becomes the default beneficiary
choices.C: Proceeds may require a guardian/custodial arrangement (or court involvement), delaying payment
choices.D: The insurer will refuse to pay any claim under any circumstances

questions.rationale: Minors generally cannot legally manage proceeds; a guardian/custodian or trust is commonly needed.

choices.D: The policy becomes paid-up automatically at year 10

questions.rationale: UL sustainability depends on funding, charges, and crediting; underfunding can lead to lapse.

questions.question: Which disclosure is MOST important for a variable life policy?

choices.B: Cash value is guaranteed not to decline
choices.C: Cash value (and some death benefit features) can fluctuate with market performance; investment risk is borne by the owner
choices.D: The policy eliminates all taxes

questions.rationale: Variable products are securities-like; values fluctuate and risk is shifted to the policyowner.

questions.question: A participating whole life policy pays dividends. If the owner chooses 'paid-up additions,' what is the most likely effect?

choices.B: Converts the policy to term automatically
choices.C: Guarantees dividends in the future
choices.D: Increases cash value and death benefit over time by purchasing additional permanent insurance

questions.rationale: Paid-up additions use dividends to buy extra permanent coverage, increasing both cash value and death benefit.

questions.question: A whole life policyowner stops paying premiums and wants to keep the same face amount for as long as possible using existing cash value. Which nonforfeiture option fits BEST?

choices.C: Accidental death benefit rider
choices.D: Reduced paid-up insurance

questions.rationale: Extended term uses cash value to buy term coverage for the original face amount for a limited time.

questions.question: A waiver of premium rider typically does what when the insured becomes totally disabled (per rider definition)?

choices.A: Converts the policy to an annuity immediately
choices.C: Eliminates the suicide clause
choices.D: Waives premiums after an elimination period to keep the policy in force

questions.rationale: Waiver riders generally waive required premiums after a waiting (elimination) period during qualifying disability.

questions.question: To start a life insurance claim, which items are most commonly required? (Select all that apply.)

choices.C: New medical exam of the beneficiary
choices.D: Additional documentation depending on circumstances (e.g., accident reports)

questions.rationale: Claims generally require a claim form and certified death certificate; additional documents may be needed depending on the loss.

questions.question: An agent offers to pay a client's first premium if the client buys today. This is most likely:

choices.C: A permissible discount in all cases
choices.D: A required replacement disclosure

questions.rationale: Providing something of value contingent on purchase (like paying premium) is commonly prohibited as rebating/inducement.

questions.question: A policy is assigned as collateral for a bank loan. If the insured dies with the debt outstanding, what happens?

choices.B: The beneficiary is paid first and the lender receives nothing
choices.C: The agent distributes proceeds at discretion
choices.D: The lender is paid up to the debt amount first; the remainder is paid to the beneficiary

questions.rationale: Collateral assignments give the lender priority up to the amount owed; the beneficiary receives the remainder.

questions.question: Life insurance death proceeds are generally income-tax-free to beneficiaries, but which item is typically taxable?

choices.A: The face amount paid as a lump sum
choices.B: The beneficiary's relationship to the insured
choices.C: The policy's free-look period
choices.D: Interest earned if proceeds are held by the insurer under an interest option

questions.rationale: The death benefit is generally income-tax-free; however, interest earned on held proceeds is typically taxable.

questions.question: For a nonqualified deferred annuity, withdrawals before annuitization are generally taxed how?

choices.C: Earnings-first (LIFO) until earnings are withdrawn
choices.D: Basis-first (FIFO) until basis is withdrawn

questions.rationale: Nonqualified annuity withdrawals are commonly treated as LIFO (earnings first), making early withdrawals taxable.

questions.question: Two partners want funds so the surviving partner can buy the deceased partner’s business interest. What is the best solution?

choices.A: A buy-sell agreement funded by life insurance
choices.C: Credit life insurance
choices.D: Accidental death rider only

questions.rationale: Buy-sell funding provides liquidity for ownership transfer on death.

questions.question: If a life insurance policy becomes a Modified Endowment Contract (MEC), the main practical impact is:

choices.C: Loans/withdrawals are taxed less favorably (often LIFO) and may incur additional penalties if under 59½
choices.D: Policy automatically cancels

questions.rationale: MEC status changes taxation of distributions; death benefit is generally still income-tax-free.

questions.question: Riley wants to buy a life insurance policy on Cameron, who is not related and has no business relationship. What is the most likely underwriting/legal issue?

choices.A: The policy would be valid because insurable interest is only checked at claim time
choices.B: Lack of insurable interest at inception (wagering-type arrangement)
choices.C: The policy is automatically valid if premiums are paid annually
choices.D: The only issue is the beneficiary designation being revocable

questions.rationale: Life insurance generally requires insurable interest at inception; otherwise it resembles a wagering contract.

questions.question: Jordan is considering replacing an existing life policy with a new one because an agent says it's 'cheaper.' What is the BEST next step to reduce harm and stay compliant?

choices.A: Backdate the new policy to eliminate contestability
choices.B: Provide required replacement notices/comparisons and avoid canceling the old policy until the new policy is issued, delivered, and in force
choices.C: Promise guaranteed savings to help the client decide
choices.D: Surrender the old policy immediately to avoid paying two premiums

questions.rationale: Replacements are regulated and can create gaps; proper disclosures and sequencing (new coverage in force first) are key.

questions.question: Riley names a minor child as the sole beneficiary with no trust/custodian named. If Riley dies while the child is still a minor, what is the most likely complication?

choices.A: The agent becomes the default beneficiary
choices.B: The minor automatically receives and manages proceeds immediately with full legal capacity
choices.C: The insurer will refuse to pay any claim under any circumstances
choices.D: Proceeds may require a guardian/custodial arrangement (or court involvement), delaying payment

questions.rationale: Minors generally cannot legally manage proceeds; a guardian/custodian or trust is commonly needed.

questions.question: Which policy provision states that the policy (and attached application/riders, if any) constitutes the full agreement between insurer and owner?

choices.C: Incontestability provision
choices.D: Insuring clause

questions.rationale: The entire contract clause limits the agreement to the written policy and included documents—not verbal promises.

questions.question: Which feature is MOST commonly guaranteed in traditional whole life insurance?

choices.B: Guaranteed dividends at a fixed rate
choices.C: Premiums that decrease automatically each year
choices.D: A guaranteed cash value schedule and level premium per the contract

questions.rationale: Whole life typically guarantees level premiums and cash values; dividends (if any) are generally not guaranteed.

questions.question: A client wants the lowest-cost temporary death benefit protection for 20 years (income replacement while children are young). What is the BEST fit?

choices.C: Immediate annuity
choices.D: Variable life for growth potential

questions.rationale: Level term is usually the lowest-cost way to buy a large temporary death benefit for a defined period.

questions.question: A universal life (UL) policyowner pays the minimum premium for years. COI charges rise with age and credited interest falls. What is the most likely risk?

choices.A: Cash value may be depleted and the policy can lapse if funding is insufficient
choices.B: The policy becomes paid-up automatically at year 10
choices.C: The insurer must reduce charges to match the premium
choices.D: Contestability restarts each year

questions.rationale: UL sustainability depends on funding, charges, and crediting; underfunding can lead to lapse.

questions.question: Which disclosure is MOST important for a variable life policy?

choices.C: Cash value is guaranteed not to decline
choices.D: Cash value (and some death benefit features) can fluctuate with market performance; investment risk is borne by the owner

questions.rationale: Variable products are securities-like; values fluctuate and risk is shifted to the policyowner.
